title: Sample and population standard deviation
description: Practice calculating sample and population standard deviation.
topic_path: Variance and standard deviation of a sample

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of all $6$ lions at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad13,\enspace 2,\enspace 1,\enspace 5,\enspace 2,\enspace 7$

 ** What is the average age of the lions at your zoo? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we have data for all $6$ lions at the zoo, we are able to calculate the population mean $(\blue{\mu})$ and population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$.

To find the population mean, add up the values of all $6$ ages and divide by $6$. 

  $ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} x_i}{\green{N}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{6}} x_i}{\green{6}} $
$ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{13 + 2 + 1 + 5 + 2 + 7}{\green{6}} \quad = \quad \blue{5\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each lion. 

 Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\mu})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\mu})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $13$ years | $8$ years | $64$ years$^2$ 
 $2$ years | $-3$ years | $9$ years$^2$ 
 $1$ year | $-4$ years | $16$ years$^2$ 
 $5$ years | $0$ years | $0$ years$^2$ 
 $2$ years | $-3$ years | $9$ years$^2$ 
 $7$ years | $2$ years | $4$ years$^2$

Because we used the population mean$(\blue{\mu})$ to compute the squared deviations from the mean, we can find the variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$, without introducing any bias, by simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean: 

  $ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} (x_i - \blue{\mu})^2}{\green{N}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{64} + \color{purple}{9} + \color{purple}{16} + \color{purple}{0} + \color{purple}{9} + \color{purple}{4}} {\green{6}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{102}}{\green{6}} \quad = \quad \red{17\text{ years}^2} $

As you might guess from the notation, the population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$ is found by taking the square root of the population variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$. 

  $\pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{\sigma^2}}$
$ \pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{17\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{4.1\text{ years}} $
The average lion at the zoo is $5$ years old. There is a standard deviation of $4.1$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of all $5$ gorillas at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad8,\enspace 4,\enspace 14,\enspace 16,\enspace 8$

 ** What is the average age of the gorillas at your zoo? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we have data for all $5$ gorillas at the zoo, we are able to calculate the population mean $(\blue{\mu})$ and population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$.

To find the population mean, add up the values of all $5$ ages and divide by $5$. 

  $ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} x_i}{\green{N}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{5}} x_i}{\green{5}} $
$ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{8 + 4 + 14 + 16 + 8}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \blue{10\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each gorilla. 

 Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\mu})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\mu})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $8$ years | $-2$ years | $4$ years$^2$ 
 $4$ years | $-6$ years | $36$ years$^2$ 
 $14$ years | $4$ years | $16$ years$^2$ 
 $16$ years | $6$ years | $36$ years$^2$ 
 $8$ years | $-2$ years | $4$ years$^2$

Because we used the population mean$(\blue{\mu})$ to compute the squared deviations from the mean, we can find the variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$, without introducing any bias, by simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean: 

  $ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} (x_i - \blue{\mu})^2}{\green{N}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{4} + \color{purple}{36} + \color{purple}{16} + \color{purple}{36} + \color{purple}{4}} {\green{5}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{96}}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \red{19.2\text{ years}^2} $

As you might guess from the notation, the population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$ is found by taking the square root of the population variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$. 

  $\pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{\sigma^2}}$
$ \pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{19.2\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{4.4\text{ years}} $
The average gorilla at the zoo is $10$ years old. There is a standard deviation of $4.4$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of all $4$ bears at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad5,\enspace 4,\enspace 6,\enspace 39$

 ** What is the average age of the bears at your zoo? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we have data for all $4$ bears at the zoo, we are able to calculate the population mean $(\blue{\mu})$ and population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$.

To find the population mean, add up the values of all $4$ ages and divide by $4$. 

  $ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} x_i}{\green{N}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{4}} x_i}{\green{4}} $
$ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{5 + 4 + 6 + 39}{\green{4}} \quad = \quad \blue{13.5\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each bear. 

 Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\mu})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\mu})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $5$ years | $-8.5$ years | $72.25$ years$^2$ 
 $4$ years | $-9.5$ years | $90.25$ years$^2$ 
 $6$ years | $-7.5$ years | $56.25$ years$^2$ 
 $39$ years | $25.5$ years | $650.25$ years$^2$

Because we used the population mean$(\blue{\mu})$ to compute the squared deviations from the mean, we can find the variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$, without introducing any bias, by simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean: 

  $ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} (x_i - \blue{\mu})^2}{\green{N}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{72.25} + \color{purple}{90.25} + \color{purple}{56.25} + \color{purple}{650.25}} {\green{4}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{869}}{\green{4}} \quad = \quad \red{217.25\text{ years}^2} $

As you might guess from the notation, the population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$ is found by taking the square root of the population variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$. 

  $\pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{\sigma^2}}$
$ \pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{217.25\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{14.7\text{ years}} $
The average bear at the zoo is $13.5$ years old. There is a standard deviation of $14.7$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of all $6$ lizards at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad1,\enspace 2,\enspace 2,\enspace 1,\enspace 3,\enspace 3$

 ** What is the average age of the lizards at your zoo? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we have data for all $6$ lizards at the zoo, we are able to calculate the population mean $(\blue{\mu})$ and population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$.

To find the population mean, add up the values of all $6$ ages and divide by $6$. 

  $ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} x_i}{\green{N}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{6}} x_i}{\green{6}} $
$ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 3 + 3}{\green{6}} \quad = \quad \blue{2\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each lizard. 

 Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\mu})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\mu})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $1$ year | $-1$ years | $1$ year$^2$ 
 $2$ years | $0$ years | $0$ years$^2$ 
 $2$ years | $0$ years | $0$ years$^2$ 
 $1$ year | $-1$ years | $1$ year$^2$ 
 $3$ years | $1$ year | $1$ year$^2$ 
 $3$ years | $1$ year | $1$ year$^2$

Because we used the population mean$(\blue{\mu})$ to compute the squared deviations from the mean, we can find the variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$, without introducing any bias, by simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean: 

  $ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} (x_i - \blue{\mu})^2}{\green{N}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{1} + \color{purple}{0} + \color{purple}{0} + \color{purple}{1} + \color{purple}{1} + \color{purple}{1}} {\green{6}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{4}}{\green{6}} \quad = \quad \red{0.67\text{ years}^2} $

As you might guess from the notation, the population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$ is found by taking the square root of the population variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$. 

  $\pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{\sigma^2}}$
$ \pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{0.67\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{0.8\text{ years}} $
The average lizard at the zoo is $2$ years old. There is a standard deviation of $0.8$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of all $5$ zebras at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad8,\enspace 11,\enspace 17,\enspace 7,\enspace 19$

 ** What is the average age of the zebras at your zoo? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we have data for all $5$ zebras at the zoo, we are able to calculate the population mean $(\blue{\mu})$ and population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$.

To find the population mean, add up the values of all $5$ ages and divide by $5$. 

  $ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} x_i}{\green{N}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{5}} x_i}{\green{5}} $
$ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{8 + 11 + 17 + 7 + 19}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \blue{12.4\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each zebra. 

 Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\mu})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\mu})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $8$ years | $-4.4$ years | $19.36$ years$^2$ 
 $11$ years | $-1.4$ years | $1.96$ years$^2$ 
 $17$ years | $4.6$ years | $21.16$ years$^2$ 
 $7$ years | $-5.4$ years | $29.16$ years$^2$ 
 $19$ years | $6.6$ years | $43.56$ years$^2$

Because we used the population mean$(\blue{\mu})$ to compute the squared deviations from the mean, we can find the variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$, without introducing any bias, by simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean: 

  $ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} (x_i - \blue{\mu})^2}{\green{N}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{19.36} + \color{purple}{1.96} + \color{purple}{21.16} + \color{purple}{29.16} + \color{purple}{43.56}} {\green{5}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{115.2}}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \red{23.04\text{ years}^2} $

As you might guess from the notation, the population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$ is found by taking the square root of the population variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$. 

  $\pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{\sigma^2}}$
$ \pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{23.04\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{4.8\text{ years}} $
The average zebra at the zoo is $12.4$ years old. There is a standard deviation of $4.8$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of all $5$ porcupines at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad16,\enspace 10,\enspace 5,\enspace 7,\enspace 13$

 ** What is the average age of the porcupines at your zoo? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we have data for all $5$ porcupines at the zoo, we are able to calculate the population mean $(\blue{\mu})$ and population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$.

To find the population mean, add up the values of all $5$ ages and divide by $5$. 

  $ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} x_i}{\green{N}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{5}} x_i}{\green{5}} $
$ \blue{\mu} \quad = \quad \dfrac{16 + 10 + 5 + 7 + 13}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \blue{10.2\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each porcupine. 

 Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\mu})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\mu})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $16$ years | $5.8$ years | $33.64$ years$^2$ 
 $10$ years | $-0.2$ years | $0.04$ years$^2$ 
 $5$ years | $-5.2$ years | $27.04$ years$^2$ 
 $7$ years | $-3.2$ years | $10.24$ years$^2$ 
 $13$ years | $2.8$ years | $7.84$ years$^2$

Because we used the population mean$(\blue{\mu})$ to compute the squared deviations from the mean, we can find the variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$, without introducing any bias, by simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean: 

  $ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{N}} (x_i - \blue{\mu})^2}{\green{N}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{33.64} + \color{purple}{0.04} + \color{purple}{27.04} + \color{purple}{10.24} + \color{purple}{7.84}} {\green{5}} $
$ \red{\sigma^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{78.8}}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \red{15.76\text{ years}^2} $

As you might guess from the notation, the population standard deviation $(\pink{\sigma})$ is found by taking the square root of the population variance $(\red{\sigma^2})$. 

  $\pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{\sigma^2}}$
$ \pink{\sigma} = \sqrt{\red{15.76\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{4\text{ years}} $
The average porcupine at the zoo is $10.2$ years old. There is a standard deviation of $4$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of $6$ lions. Those lions were randomly selected from the $22$ lions at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad13,\enspace 2,\enspace 1,\enspace 5,\enspace 2,\enspace 7$

 ** Based on your sample, what is the average age of the lions? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we only have data for a small sample of the $22$ lions, we are only able to estimate the population mean and standard deviation by finding the sample mean $(\blue{\overline{x}})$ and sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$.

To find the sample mean, add up the values of all $6$ samples and divide by $6$. 

  $ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} x_i}{\green{n}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{6}} x_i}{\green{6}} $
$ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{13 + 2 + 1 + 5 + 2 + 7}{\green{6}} \quad = \quad \blue{5\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each sample. Since we don't know the population mean, estimate the mean by using the sample mean we just calculated $(\blue{\overline{x}} = \blue{5\text{ years}})$.

Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $13$ years | $8$ years | $64$ years$^2$ 
 $2$ years | $-3$ years | $9$ years$^2$ 
 $1$ year | $-4$ years | $16$ years$^2$ 
 $5$ years | $0$ years | $0$ years$^2$ 
 $2$ years | $-3$ years | $9$ years$^2$ 
 $7$ years | $2$ years | $4$ years$^2$

Normally we can find the variance $(\red{s^2})$ by averaging the squared deviations from the mean. But remember we don't know the real population mean—we had to estimate it by using the sample mean. 

  The age of any particular lion in our sample is likely to be closer to the average age of the $6$ lions we sampled. This is compared to the average age of all $22$ lions in the zoo. Because of that, the squared deviations from the mean we calculated will probably underestimate the actual deviations from the population mean. 

  To compensate for this underestimation, rather than simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean, we total them and divide by $n - 1$. 

  $ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} (x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2}{\green{n - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{64} + \color{purple}{9} + \color{purple}{16} + \color{purple}{0} + \color{purple}{9} + \color{purple}{4}} {\green{6 - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{102}}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \red{20.4\text{ years}^2} $

The sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$ is found by taking the square root of the sample variance $(\red{s^2})$. 

  $\pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{s^2}}$
$ \pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{20.4\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{4.5\text{ years}} $
We can estimate that the average lion at the zoo is $5$ years old.  There is also a standard deviation of $4.5$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of $5$ gorillas. Those gorillas were randomly selected from the $29$ gorillas at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad8,\enspace 4,\enspace 14,\enspace 16,\enspace 8$

 ** Based on your sample, what is the average age of the gorillas? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we only have data for a small sample of the $29$ gorillas, we are only able to estimate the population mean and standard deviation by finding the sample mean $(\blue{\overline{x}})$ and sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$.

To find the sample mean, add up the values of all $5$ samples and divide by $5$. 

  $ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} x_i}{\green{n}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{5}} x_i}{\green{5}} $
$ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{8 + 4 + 14 + 16 + 8}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \blue{10\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each sample. Since we don't know the population mean, estimate the mean by using the sample mean we just calculated $(\blue{\overline{x}} = \blue{10\text{ years}})$.

Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $8$ years | $-2$ years | $4$ years$^2$ 
 $4$ years | $-6$ years | $36$ years$^2$ 
 $14$ years | $4$ years | $16$ years$^2$ 
 $16$ years | $6$ years | $36$ years$^2$ 
 $8$ years | $-2$ years | $4$ years$^2$

Normally we can find the variance $(\red{s^2})$ by averaging the squared deviations from the mean. But remember we don't know the real population mean—we had to estimate it by using the sample mean. 

  The age of any particular gorilla in our sample is likely to be closer to the average age of the $5$ gorillas we sampled. This is compared to the average age of all $29$ gorillas in the zoo. Because of that, the squared deviations from the mean we calculated will probably underestimate the actual deviations from the population mean. 

  To compensate for this underestimation, rather than simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean, we total them and divide by $n - 1$. 

  $ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} (x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2}{\green{n - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{4} + \color{purple}{36} + \color{purple}{16} + \color{purple}{36} + \color{purple}{4}} {\green{5 - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{96}}{\green{4}} \quad = \quad \red{24\text{ years}^2} $

The sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$ is found by taking the square root of the sample variance $(\red{s^2})$. 

  $\pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{s^2}}$
$ \pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{24\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{4.9\text{ years}} $
We can estimate that the average gorilla at the zoo is $10$ years old.  There is also a standard deviation of $4.9$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of $4$ bears. Those bears were randomly selected from the $22$ bears at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad5,\enspace 4,\enspace 6,\enspace 39$

 ** Based on your sample, what is the average age of the bears? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we only have data for a small sample of the $22$ bears, we are only able to estimate the population mean and standard deviation by finding the sample mean $(\blue{\overline{x}})$ and sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$.

To find the sample mean, add up the values of all $4$ samples and divide by $4$. 

  $ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} x_i}{\green{n}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{4}} x_i}{\green{4}} $
$ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{5 + 4 + 6 + 39}{\green{4}} \quad = \quad \blue{13.5\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each sample. Since we don't know the population mean, estimate the mean by using the sample mean we just calculated $(\blue{\overline{x}} = \blue{13.5\text{ years}})$.

Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $5$ years | $-8.5$ years | $72.25$ years$^2$ 
 $4$ years | $-9.5$ years | $90.25$ years$^2$ 
 $6$ years | $-7.5$ years | $56.25$ years$^2$ 
 $39$ years | $25.5$ years | $650.25$ years$^2$

Normally we can find the variance $(\red{s^2})$ by averaging the squared deviations from the mean. But remember we don't know the real population mean—we had to estimate it by using the sample mean. 

  The age of any particular bear in our sample is likely to be closer to the average age of the $4$ bears we sampled. This is compared to the average age of all $22$ bears in the zoo. Because of that, the squared deviations from the mean we calculated will probably underestimate the actual deviations from the population mean. 

  To compensate for this underestimation, rather than simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean, we total them and divide by $n - 1$. 

  $ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} (x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2}{\green{n - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{72.25} + \color{purple}{90.25} + \color{purple}{56.25} + \color{purple}{650.25}} {\green{4 - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{869}}{\green{3}} \quad = \quad \red{289.67\text{ years}^2} $

The sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$ is found by taking the square root of the sample variance $(\red{s^2})$. 

  $\pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{s^2}}$
$ \pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{289.67\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{17\text{ years}} $
We can estimate that the average bear at the zoo is $13.5$ years old.  There is also a standard deviation of $17$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of $6$ lizards. Those lizards were randomly selected from the $36$ lizards at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad1,\enspace 2,\enspace 2,\enspace 1,\enspace 3,\enspace 3$

 ** Based on your sample, what is the average age of the lizards? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we only have data for a small sample of the $36$ lizards, we are only able to estimate the population mean and standard deviation by finding the sample mean $(\blue{\overline{x}})$ and sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$.

To find the sample mean, add up the values of all $6$ samples and divide by $6$. 

  $ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} x_i}{\green{n}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{6}} x_i}{\green{6}} $
$ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 3 + 3}{\green{6}} \quad = \quad \blue{2\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each sample. Since we don't know the population mean, estimate the mean by using the sample mean we just calculated $(\blue{\overline{x}} = \blue{2\text{ years}})$.

Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $1$ year | $-1$ years | $1$ year$^2$ 
 $2$ years | $0$ years | $0$ years$^2$ 
 $2$ years | $0$ years | $0$ years$^2$ 
 $1$ year | $-1$ years | $1$ year$^2$ 
 $3$ years | $1$ year | $1$ year$^2$ 
 $3$ years | $1$ year | $1$ year$^2$

Normally we can find the variance $(\red{s^2})$ by averaging the squared deviations from the mean. But remember we don't know the real population mean—we had to estimate it by using the sample mean. 

  The age of any particular lizard in our sample is likely to be closer to the average age of the $6$ lizards we sampled. This is compared to the average age of all $36$ lizards in the zoo. Because of that, the squared deviations from the mean we calculated will probably underestimate the actual deviations from the population mean. 

  To compensate for this underestimation, rather than simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean, we total them and divide by $n - 1$. 

  $ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} (x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2}{\green{n - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{1} + \color{purple}{0} + \color{purple}{0} + \color{purple}{1} + \color{purple}{1} + \color{purple}{1}} {\green{6 - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{4}}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \red{0.8\text{ years}^2} $

The sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$ is found by taking the square root of the sample variance $(\red{s^2})$. 

  $\pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{s^2}}$
$ \pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{0.8\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{0.9\text{ years}} $
We can estimate that the average lizard at the zoo is $2$ years old.  There is also a standard deviation of $0.9$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of $5$ zebras. Those zebras were randomly selected from the $42$ zebras at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad8,\enspace 11,\enspace 17,\enspace 7,\enspace 19$

 ** Based on your sample, what is the average age of the zebras? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we only have data for a small sample of the 42 zebras, we are only able to estimate the population mean and standard deviation by finding the sample mean $(\blue{\overline{x}})$ and sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$.

To find the sample mean, add up the values of all $5$ samples and divide by $5$. 

  $ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} x_i}{\green{n}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{5}} x_i}{\green{5}} $
$ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{8 + 11 + 17 + 7 + 19}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \blue{12.4\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each sample. Since we don't know the population mean, estimate the mean by using the sample mean we just calculated $(\blue{\overline{x}} = \blue{12.4\text{ years}})$.

Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $8$ years | $-4.4$ years | $19.36$ years$^2$ 
 $11$ years | $-1.4$ years | $1.96$ years$^2$ 
 $17$ years | $4.6$ years | $21.16$ years$^2$ 
 $7$ years | $-5.4$ years | $29.16$ years$^2$ 
 $19$ years | $6.6$ years | $43.56$ years$^2$

Normally we can find the variance $(\red{s^2})$ by averaging the squared deviations from the mean. But remember we don't know the real population mean—we had to estimate it by using the sample mean. 

  The age of any particular zebra in our sample is likely to be closer to the average age of the 5 zebras we sampled. This is compared to the average age of all $42$ zebras in the zoo. Because of that, the squared deviations from the mean we calculated will probably underestimate the actual deviations from the population mean. 

  To compensate for this underestimation, rather than simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean, we total them and divide by $n - 1$. 

  $ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} (x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2}{\green{n - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{19.36} + \color{purple}{1.96} + \color{purple}{21.16} + \color{purple}{29.16} + \color{purple}{43.56}} {\green{5 - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{115.2}}{\green{4}} \quad = \quad \red{28.8\text{ years}^2} $

The sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$ is found by taking the square root of the sample variance $(\red{s^2})$. 

  $\pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{s^2}}$
$ \pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{28.8\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{5.4\text{ years}} $
We can estimate that the average zebra at the zoo is $12.4$ years old.  There is also a standard deviation of $5.4$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
You have found the following ages (in years) of $5$ porcupines. Those porcupines were randomly selected from the $26$ porcupines at your local zoo: 

 $\qquad16,\enspace 10,\enspace 5,\enspace 7,\enspace 13$

 ** Based on your sample, what is the average age of the porcupines? What is the standard deviation? Round your answers to the nearest tenth. **

Average age:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] years old 

  Standard deviation:
 
$\quad$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] years
hints: Because we only have data for a small sample of the $26$ porcupines, we are only able to estimate the population mean and standard deviation by finding the sample mean $(\blue{\overline{x}})$ and sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$.

To find the sample mean, add up the values of all $5$ samples and divide by $5$. 

  $ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} x_i}{\green{n}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{5}} x_i}{\green{5}} $
$ \blue{\overline{x}} \quad = \quad \dfrac{16 + 10 + 5 + 7 + 13}{\green{5}} \quad = \quad \blue{10.2\text{ years old}} $

Find the squared deviations from the mean for each sample. Since we don't know the population mean, estimate the mean by using the sample mean we just calculated $(\blue{\overline{x}} = \blue{10.2\text{ years}})$.

Age
 
 $x_i$ | Distance from the mean $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})$ | $(x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2$ 
-|-|-
 $16$ years | $5.8$ years | $33.64$ years$^2$ 
 $10$ years | $-0.2$ years | $0.04$ years$^2$ 
 $5$ years | $-5.2$ years | $27.04$ years$^2$ 
 $7$ years | $-3.2$ years | $10.24$ years$^2$ 
 $13$ years | $2.8$ years | $7.84$ years$^2$

Normally we can find the variance $(\red{s^2})$ by averaging the squared deviations from the mean. But remember we don't know the real population mean—we had to estimate it by using the sample mean. 

  The age of any particular porcupine in our sample is likely to be closer to the average age of the 5 porcupines we sampled. This is compared to the average age of all 26 porcupines in the zoo. Because of that, the squared deviations from the mean we calculated will probably underestimate the actual deviations from the population mean. 

  To compensate for this underestimation, rather than simply averaging the squared deviations from the mean, we total them and divide by $n - 1$. 

  $ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\green{n}} (x_i - \blue{\overline{x}})^2}{\green{n - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\color{purple}{33.64} + \color{purple}{0.04} + \color{purple}{27.04} + \color{purple}{10.24} + \color{purple}{7.84}} {\green{5 - 1}} $
$ \red{s^2} \quad = \quad \dfrac{\purple{78.8}}{\green{4}} \quad = \quad \red{19.7\text{ years}^2} $

The sample standard deviation $(\pink{s})$ is found by taking the square root of the sample variance $(\red{s^2})$. 

  $\pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{s^2}}$
$ \pink{s} = \sqrt{\red{19.7\text{ years}^2}} = \pink{4.4\text{ years}} $
We can estimate that the average porcupine at the zoo is $10.2$ years old.  There is also a standard deviation of $4.4$ years.
